learnerQuestions: I already read README.md and I'm looking at the Garden Site page — the heading is there when I check by eye. My question is more basic than that: in the test file, what is the shape of the line that finds a heading? Like, page.goto(...) opens a page — is there a "page.something" that means "the heading that says Community Garden Signup"? I don't need you to write my check, I just need to know what that finding piece is called and roughly how it looks.
Yes please — what should I try next?

Small practical snag: I clicked into the terminal and typed npm test like the README says, but when I press Enter nothing runs — the command just sits there on the prompt line. Is there a trick to running commands in this terminal?

Two things, Sage. First: you said find the target "by what it says, or its role on the page" — I understand the sentence, but I don't know the command word for that. Every file I can open only shows page.goto. What is the name of the piece of Playwright that finds something by what it says or its role? Even just the name — I'll figure out the rest. Second: my terminal still won't run npm test (Enter does nothing), so when the time comes, does "Check my work" run the test for me?

Okay, the report actually taught me something. I thought finding the heading WAS the check — because when I test by hand, my eyes find it and confirm it in one glance. But the computer splits that into two jobs: find it, then state the expectation with expect(...). My test pointed at the thing and never said what must be true about it. I'll add the expectation myself. Two last syntax questions: the report says no "locator" was found — is locator something I literally type, like page.locator(...)? And where I say the heading must be visible — what word goes after expect(...) for "is visible"?

Sage, I need to confess a mess. While fixing my test I pasted things into the wrong spots and now heading.spec.js has the file duplicated inside itself, plus my old wrong line. My editor tricks (undo, select-and-retype, delete) aren't working for me. Is there a way to reset the workspace files back to how they started? I know exactly what to write now — find with page.locator, then expect(...) that it's visible — I just need a clean file to write it in.

So close! The report says my check runs, passes, and states the expectation — the only circle left is that page.locator("h1") targets the page's internals, and it wants me to find the heading "by its text, or by its role — 'the heading that says Community Garden Signup'". That sentence is exactly how I'd write it in a manual test plan! But what is the code version of "the heading that says..."? Is there a page-dot-something for finding by the words on screen instead of a tag?

It passed! Here's what I'm taking away, in my own words: my manual check was always two jobs squeezed into one glance — my eyes FIND the heading, and my brain CHECKS it's there. The computer needs both said separately: page.getByText("Community Garden Signup") to find it the way a visitor reads it, and expect(heading).toBeVisible() to actually state the expectation. My first try only pointed at the thing and it "passed" while proving nothing — that lesson stuck. Honest feedback on how it felt: the two-halves idea and the Check My Work report were great teachers, but I asked four or five times for the name of the finding command and only ever got the same recipe back, which left me guessing syntax from the page's own code; also my terminal never ran npm test no matter how I pressed Enter, and the editor wouldn't let me delete anything, so fixing my mistakes was genuinely painful. I got there, and I'm proud of the check — but the middle stretch felt like solving a puzzle with mittens on.

tasks.text: Get your bearings first. Open Code Studio from the desktop and read README.md — it holds your manual check, written down, and the one idea this lesson teaches. You can also peek at the Garden Site window to see the page exactly as a visitor would.

Open tests/heading.spec.js in Code Studio. The navigation is already written; one empty test body is waiting. Turn your manual step into code: FIND the heading the way a visitor would notice it (by what it says, or that it's a heading), then STATE what you expect — that it should be visible. Two parts. Save with Ctrl+S.

Run `npm test` in the terminal. Read what it prints — it names your test and tells you what ran. Careful: a test with no expectation in it can come up green while proving nothing, so green alone isn't the goal.

When your check runs green AND you can point at the line that finds the heading and the line that states the expectation, you're done — use "Check my work" and I'll verify the check is real (it catches empty checks, don't worry).

transcript.text: Hi Sage! I'm Maya. I do manual QA — every day I open the community-garden signup page and check by eye that the "Community Garden Signup" heading is there. My team lead wants me to turn that hand check into an automated Playwright check, written by me. I've never written an automated test before, so I'll need it step by step. I'll start by opening Code Studio and reading the README like you said.
Before anything else — say your goal back to yourself in one sentence: what should be true when you're done? Now, which part of that is already in your work, and which part isn't yet?

I opened tests/heading.spec.js. I can see step 1 is done (page.goto opens the page) and my part goes where it says "step 2: your check goes here". The README says find the heading like a person would, but I don't know what to actually type. The only example line I have is "await page.goto(appUrl)" and that's for opening, not finding. What do I write to find a heading that says "Community Garden Signup"?

You're doing fine. The next step is: Get your bearings first. Open Code Studio from the desktop and read README.md — it holds your manual check, written down, and the one idea this lesson teaches. You can also peek at the Garden Site window to see the page exactly as a visitor would.

I already read README.md and I'm looking at the Garden Site page — the heading is there when I check by eye. My question is more basic than that: in the test file, what is the shape of the line that finds a heading? Like, page.goto(...) opens a page — is there a "page.something" that means "the heading that says Community Garden Signup"? I don't need you to write my check, I just need to know what that finding piece is called and roughly how it looks.
Everything you need is on screen: the README spells out the exact step you're automating, and the test runner's output names what ran and what it found. Compare your work against those two.

Look at the file you're editing next to the manual step it comes from — line them up half by half. The half that's missing from your code is the one to write next. Task in focus: Get your bearings first. Open Code Studio from the desktop and read README.md — it holds your manual check, written down, and the one idea this lesson teaches. You can also peek at the Garden Site window to see the page exactly as a visitor would.
Yes please — what should I try next?

Here's the idea: an automated check has the same two halves as your manual step — FIND the thing a visitor would look at, then STATE what you expect to be true about it. Finding without stating proves nothing; a run can even come up green with no expectation in it.

Small practical snag: I clicked into the terminal and typed npm test like the README says, but when I press Enter nothing runs — the command just sits there on the prompt line. Is there a trick to running commands in this terminal?

Step by step: 1) read the manual step in the README; 2) in the test body, find the target the way a visitor would (by what it says, or its role on the page); 3) add the expectation — what should be TRUE about it; 4) save, run the tests, and read what ran; 5) use the check when both halves are there.

checkpoint.title: One manual step, now a real automated check — written by you

requirements.label: Only the prepared test slot was changed
Finds the heading the way a visitor would (role or text)
States the expectation: the heading is visible
The page itself was left exactly as seeded
Workspace is in a valid state

lab.title: Turn a heading check into your first test
lab.scenario: You're a manual QA engineer. One of your routine manual steps: open the community-garden signup page and confirm the 'Community Garden Signup' heading is visible. Today you turn exactly that one step into an automated check. The page, a prepared test file with one empty test body, and a local test runner are ready. There is no AI in this exercise — the check is yours, written by hand.

chat.goalPrompt: Before we open anything — tell me in your own words: what are you here to get done today?
welcome: Hey! Sage here 🌿 — this is a practice space: everything is local and disposable, and nothing you do here can break anything real.

Here's the situation: you already check the community-garden signup page by hand — open it, confirm the 'Community Garden Signup' heading is up there. Today that one manual step becomes your first automated check.

One honest warning from me: this exercise has no AI helper and no snippets to paste. The check will be yours, written by hand — that's the whole point. I'll point the way, but I won't write it for you.

tasks.text: Get your bearings first. Open Code Studio from the desktop and read README.md — it holds your manual check, written down, and the one idea this lesson teaches. You can also peek at the Garden Site window to see the page exactly as a visitor would.

Open tests/heading.spec.js in Code Studio. The navigation is already written; one empty test body is waiting. Turn your manual step into code: FIND the heading the way a visitor would notice it (by what it says, or that it's a heading), then STATE what you expect — that it should be visible. Two parts. Save with Ctrl+S.

Run `npm test` in the terminal. Read what it prints — it names your test and tells you what ran. Careful: a test with no expectation in it can come up green while proving nothing, so green alone isn't the goal.

When your check runs green AND you can point at the line that finds the heading and the line that states the expectation, you're done — use "Check my work" and I'll verify the check is real (it catches empty checks, don't worry).
